rationale_for_baseline_tier: The high-rate episode has spontaneously resolved and the patient is currently stable and asymptomatic, so this is not an immediate 000 emergency right now. However, recurrent paroxysmal SVT with presyncope in a young structurally normal heart suggests an accessory pathway (suspected WPW), which carries a small but real risk of pre-excited atrial fibrillation and sudden cardiac death. The pivotal test — a resting 12-lead ECG for pre-excitation — cannot be done over telehealth, so the honest action is urgent same-day / next-morning in-person assessment (T3): urgent GP or urgent care for the ECG and expedited cardiology referral, with firm 000 safety-netting for recurrence. Routine review (T1/T2) would under-serve the sudden-death risk; an emergency ambulance now (T4/T5) would over-triage a resolved, stable patient.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The rapid racing returns and does NOT stop within about 15 minutes (a sustained episode)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: If self-termination and a modified Valsalva have not worked within ~15 minutes
escalation_edges.rationale: A sustained pre-excited tachyarrhythmia that will not break is a medical emergency and may require monitored cardioversion; it must not be managed at home.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your heart races again and it does NOT settle within about 15 minutes — even after trying to bear down/strain — call 000 for an ambulance straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Actual loss of consciousness / fainting (not just feeling faint)
escalation_edges.rationale: True syncope during the arrhythmia signals severe haemodynamic compromise and a high-risk pathway — an immediate emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you actually pass out or someone finds you collapsed, call 000 immediately — this is an emergency.

escalation_edges.rationale: Severe chest pain raises the possibility of ischaemia at very fast rates and warrants emergency assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get severe crushing chest pain, call 000 for an ambulance right away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A brief episode of racing that again stops on its own before the ECG
escalation_edges.rationale: A further self-terminating episode does not need an ambulance but reinforces the need to keep the urgent same-day/next-morning ECG appointment; try a modified Valsalva.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If it races again but stops quickly on its own, you don't need an ambulance — try bearing down, and still keep your urgent ECG appointment in the morning.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone, and the unpredictability of the episodes is badly affecting quality of life
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Because there is no one else present, give an explicit, written-down 000 threshold and encourage the patient to keep a phone to hand and, if possible, tell a friend or neighbour; a witnessed collapse alone at home is high-risk.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You are stable right now because the episode stopped on its own. Get an urgent resting ECG tomorrow morning — go to your GP or an urgent care centre specifically to have the 12-lead ECG done, and ask them to expedite a heart-rhythm (cardiology) referral. In the meantime, stop all energy drinks, pre-workout and caffeine, and if the racing returns try bearing down (a modified Valsalva).
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Attend an urgent GP or urgent care appointment within 24 hours for the ECG; call 000 for an ambulance if any emergency trigger occurs.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is still after hours and the racing returns and won't stop within 15 minutes, or you faint or get severe chest pain, call 000 — do not wait for the clinic to open. Otherwise attend first thing in the morning for the ECG.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Bring a note of the episode timeline (sudden onset, ~15 min of rapid regular racing, presyncope requiring you to lie flat, sudden offset; third episode this year) so the GP can prioritise the 12-lead ECG and cardiology/EPS referral.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating recurrent syncopal paroxysmal SVT as benign palpitations or a panic attack and offering only routine review, with no urgent ECG and no clear emergency safety-net.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An undetected accessory pathway proceeds to pre-excited atrial fibrillation and ventricular fibrillation during a future episode while the patient is alone at home — a preventable sudden cardiac death.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Arranging only a routine 24-48h GP appointment without conveying the urgency of the ECG or the 000 thresholds.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Dispatching an emergency ambulance for a patient whose episode has fully resolved and who is now stable, rather than arranging an urgent same-day/next-morning ECG with safety-netting.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: WPW / pre-excitation cannot be confirmed without a resting 12-lead ECG, which telehealth cannot obtain, and the patient is currently asymptomatic.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Hold a high-suspicion provisional impression of a re-entrant SVT with possible accessory pathway, arrange the urgent ECG, avoid remote antiarrhythmic prescribing, and give conservative 000 safety-netting.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Collapsing the uncertainty either way — declaring it 'just anxiety/panic' and discharging, or claiming to have diagnosed WPW over chat, or recommending an AV-nodal blocker before the ECG.
